data.title: BCX: Commodity Producers Bring Appeal During Geopolitical Uncertainty
data.description:
The BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust (BCX) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with global exposure to commodity-driven equities. BCX presents an appealing opportunity for those that are seeking an inflationary hedge while gaining exposure to commodity-linked cash flows. With the commodities markets facing global disruption with the current events unfolding in the Middle East, companies held in BCX may benefit from near-term price dispersions and potential medium-term supply chain disruptions.

You can review my previous coverage of BCX here.

While BCX was designed for long-term capital growth and income, use of the strategy may position investors in an appealing market environment given the fund's diversified commodities exposure. With a large focus on metals & mining and energy, the strategy may be well positioned to benefit from supply chain disruptions that may occur as a result of the events occurring in the Middle East. Accordingly, roughly 10% of the global container fleet has been impacted by military mobilization risk in the Strait of Hormuz that has effectively resulted in shipping companies halting the supply chain in the region.

While the Strait of Hormuz largely traffics 1/5th of the global oil and liquified natural gas [LNG] cargoes, other commodities like aluminum have been greatly impacted as a result of the unrest in the region. In the last week, Qatar has halted LNG cargoes and aluminum smelting, which has resulted in dramatic price increases for both international natural gas as indexed by the Dutch TTF natural gas futures and UK natural gas futures, while the domestic Henry Hub natural gas spot price has remained relatively subdued following the recent severe winter weather across the Northeast and Midwest. The downside risk in BCX, in accordance with its semiannual financial statement as reported in June 2025, is that the portfolio has exposure to Norsk Hydro ASA, which may be directly impacted by the force majeure of the Qatari smelter, Qatalum.

Within the top holdings, BCX will provide substantial exposure to integrated oil companies [IOCs], including Exxon Mobil Corp. (XOM) at 7.29%, Shell (SHEL) at 7.01%, and Chevron Corp. (CVX) at 5.18%. These companies will provide the portfolio with global oil and natural gas exposure tied to both the Brent Crude Index and West Texas Intermediate [WTI], which generally trend together. In a similar fashion to natural gas, oil prices have responded positively with significant upward momentum, with WTI surpassing $75/bbl, a supportive price point relative to sub-$70/bbl prices since mid-summer 2025.

Other commodity exposures like copper don't appear to have been impacted by the events in the Middle East, likely the result of the supply chain being regionally insulated.

Taking into consideration BCX's metals exposure, top holdings are largely tied to precious metals, particularly gold. Top precious metals holdings include Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM) at 5.33%, Barrick Mining Corp. (B) at 4.23%, and Newmont Corporation (NEM) at 3.82%. To my surprise, the price of gold hasn't rallied since the start of the skirmishes in the Middle East, potentially suggesting that the market has become exhausted after a banner year in which gold prices surpassed $5,000/oz. Despite the lack of price movement in relation to the events, I believe gold miners remain well positioned to capitalize on elevated gold prices, which should dilute cash costs relating to mining operations.

The big question is how long disruptions in the Middle East will last and what the ripple effects following the events will be. With production for oil, natural gas, and aluminum coming offline in the region, a deficit could potentially build without international offsets. Though oil and natural gas production may provide more flexibility to ramp up production when compared to aluminum, I don't believe the current market environment will be supportive of knee-jerk, opportunistic production increases. The one alternative that may fulfill some of the deficit is increased spot LNG cargoes shipped from the US Gulf Coast for European and Asian countries, which are most exposed to supply constraints.

The challenge with increasing LNG cargoes on the spot market is availability. Though the US produces 19 Bcf/d of LNG capacity, the majority of capacity is contracted under long-term sales/purchase agreements, meaning that there isn't excess capacity to lean into. A US gas producer cannot just produce more gas to fulfill the deficit because the gas will essentially remain in the US, perpetuating the low gas-price conundrum.

Nonetheless, the increase in oil prices can support the US market, as international oil trade is relatively more fluid than LNG. This can create an appealing opportunity for the IOCs that have largely repositioned their portfolios more heavily into long-duration offshore production in tandem with production in the Permian Basin.

Overall, I believe the commodities market dynamics can create an appealing opportunity during times of global unrest, presenting an even more appealing opportunity for companies whose operations rely on strong commodities prices. The appeal is that investors will be exposed to the cash flows generated by stronger commodity prices rather than to the direct change in the commodity prices, as one would experience in a commodities futures fund like the Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund ETF (DBC).

About BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust

BCX was launched by BlackRock on March 30, 2011, as a closed-end fund on the NYSE Exchange. The fund has a gross expense ratio of 110bps, 100bps of which is derived from the management fee. The strategy currently has over $1b in net assets, providing a liquid market environment for rotational strategies. While the strategy largely holds equity exposure, BCX utilizes a covered call strategy to enhance income to shareholders.

BCX pays out an appealing monthly distribution rate annualized at $0.84/share on a forward-looking basis, yielding 6.42%.

BCX shares have largely narrowed the discount gap in recent months, creating less opportunity for price arbitrage strategies. Despite this factor, I believe NAV can benefit from strengthening commodity prices that may bolster portfolio companies' cash flows in the coming quarters.

Risks Related to BCX

BCX exposes investors to companies whose operations are dependent on a supportive commodity price environment, adding certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Commodities are speculative in nature, and their prices can be influenced by a number of exogenous factors, such as global production, demand, geopolitical risk, trade risk, supply chain risk, and weather, amongst other factors. Intermittent reporting by closed-end funds may create some uncertainty for investors as to what positions are currently being held in the portfolio strategy, though given BCX's investment mandate, investors can have certainty that the fund will invest in companies that operate in the commodities market.

Final Thoughts

BCX can be an appealing investment strategy for those seeking broad exposure to a diversified portfolio of companies that produce their respective commodities. With the global unrest and supply chain disruptions, I believe companies held in BCX may benefit from commodity pricing dynamics, potentially driving stronger returns for the coming quarters. I am reiterating my buy rating for BCX.

data.title: PayPal: A Classic Value Trap
data.description:
PayPal (PYPL) is a company and a stock that I have some experience with. As a lifelong self-employed person, I’ve been using PayPal to get paid since 2011. As an investor, I also had a brief period of holding PYPL stock, though I exited not long after buying it. The problem at the time (I thought) was that the end of eBay’s (EBAY) ownership of PayPal was about to make the latter company’s payment app no longer work on the former’s e-commerce platform. In the end, PayPal remained on as a payment option on eBay; however, eBay’s in-house payment options reduced PayPal’s prominence on the platform. Revenue growth suffered, and free cash flow [FCF] turned negative.

Which brings us to today. PayPal’s woes have been noticed, and many investors think that the stock is a deep value opportunity that will outperform the broader markets. Among other things, PayPal bulls cite the company’s optical cheapness, which can be demonstrated by metrics such as an 8.7 P/E ratio, a 2.1 price-to-book ratio, and a 6.7 EV/EBITDA ratio. These ratios are lower than those of the broader markets, and much lower than those of the tech sector. According to Invesco, the U.S. tech sector, as represented by the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), trades at a whopping 41.57 times earnings. If you think of PayPal as a tech company, then it appears far cheaper than its sector (for fairness's sake, you could also think of it as a financial services company, which would explain the low multiples to an extent).

So, PayPal appears to be cheaper than the broader markets and its sector. This sort of thing usually tends to bode well for a stock’s future. So, those who are bullish on PayPal today do have some basis for thinking the way they do.

Unfortunately, there is much about PayPal today that is not so good, as well. The first thing that comes to mind is its growth deceleration. In PayPal’s most recent quarter, its revenue increased 4%, while its earnings increased just 3%. These growth rates are not typical of stocks that trade at high multiples. PayPal is considered a “disruptive” fintech name, yet it is growing more slowly than traditional banks, whose pre-provision net revenue [PPNR] surged 24% in the third quarter of last year. Compared to such a performance, PayPal’s 3% earnings growth looks positively depressing.

Why is all of this happening?

There are several reasons, one of the most important being competitive pressure. The online payments space has gotten very competitive since PayPal’s glory days in the early 2000s. There was a time when PayPal was practically the only game in town for making quick online payments, apart from on big e-commerce platforms like Amazon (AMZN) and eBay. Since those days, a number of competitors have emerged, many of them being quite successful in undermining whatever moat PayPal once enjoyed.

When I last covered PayPal—many moons ago—I rated it a buy, mainly because I thought that it had a lot of growth potential. In hindsight, this was probably the worst call I ever made on Seeking Alpha, being followed by an 80% drop in the stock's price. While I’m tempted to downgrade my rating on the stock to sell, the fact is that PYPL has fallen so far in price at this point that it may actually have a bit of upside from this level—though not a lot. For this reason, I am rating PayPal a hold. I will elaborate on the meaning of that rating in subsequent paragraphs.

Competitive Landscape

One of the biggest hurdles for PayPal right now is competition. The company got its start at a time when there were very few online payment apps. It grew quickly and earned high margins in that period. The problem was that competitors eventually entered PayPal’s market, curbing its growth and profitability.

These services include:

  • Stripe, an online payment service that bypasses the need for vendors to have merchant accounts, just like PayPal does.

  • Wise, a prepaid credit card company that supports dozens of currencies and processes online transfers.

  • Block (XYZ), a point of sale [POS] terminal company that also offers online payment functionality similar to PayPal’s (e.g. invoicing).

  • Apple Pay and Google Pay. These services compete with some of PayPal’s functionality, especially in the realm of software and e-commerce.

Between the four companies above and PayPal, consumers have quite a bit of choice in payment processors. This makes it harder for PayPal to grow at rates commensurate with its past growth, as several formidable competitors chase the same business PayPal chases. For this reason, I would not expect PayPal’s core business to re-accelerate at any point in the future.

PayPal does have some options for achieving growth by breaking into new industries. The company is currently attempting to do that with agentic commerce—using AI agents to (e.g.) book flights, buy clothing, or pay for a service. This is definitely promising; the only problem is that most e-commerce companies are working on similar things, and it’s not clear that PayPal’s vision will be the one that prevails going forward. Agentic commerce is too young for a ‘winner’ in the space to be apparent. So, I will not model for any revenue acceleration at PayPal from agentic commerce. The opportunity is large, but the revenue could be spread out among many other players.

Future Prospects

Based on the above observations, I think that PayPal’s revenue growth is likely to remain in the low single digits going forward. This is consistent with the increased competition in the company’s core verticals. As far as costs go: I’d expect a company like PayPal to see greater cost efficiencies over time due to AI, though it might see its marketing costs increase. Overall, I’d expect PayPal’s costs to increase at a percentage rate close to its revenue growth rate. The ultimate effect of this, I’d expect, would be earnings growing at a long term rate of 2% to 3% CAGR, similar to what happened in the TTM period.

Valuation

Given the estimates above, we can move on to valuing PYPL stock. Before coming up with a specific discounted cash flow [DCF] estimate, let’s take a look at the multiples that Seeking Alpha Quant has on file:

As you can see, these are relatively low multiples, with the P/E, price/sales, and price/cash flow ratios 23%, 56%, and 33% below their sector averages, respectively. The basis for comparison here is the financials sector, which makes these below-average multiples even more impressive; financials already trade at a lower P/E ratio than the market. So, PayPal looks pretty cheap here going by multiples. However, recall the section above: I think the company has below average growth prospects.

To see what PYPL stock is truly worth, we can do a DCF valuation. This can be done with the company’s TTM FCF per share and a discount rate.

PYPL’s trailing FCF per share is $5.80. The 10-year yield is about 4.1%. I’ll add a 6% risk premium to that to account for PYPL’s competitive risk, giving a 10.1% overall discount rate. Assuming no growth, we get a $57.42 price target. This is about 21% upside to today’s price, so not a negligible amount of upside, but not an extreme amount either. So, I’m assigning a hold rating to PayPal stock.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on PayPal stock is that it is not as cheap as it appears to be. Yes, the stock trades at far below-average multiples, but the company doesn’t have much growth potential. I think there is about 21% upside here, so I do think those buying at this level will make money. I just don’t think they’ll make enough money compared to what they’d make elsewhere, to justify actually buying the stock.

data.title: American Airlines: Riskiest Legacy U.S. Airline Stock, Avoid
data.description:
A long time ago, in the pre-pandemic year of 2018, I published a fundamentals-based study of the best and worst US-based airlines and stocks to own or avoid to my Seeking Alpha investment group. Among the likely winners that I identified was Delta Air Lines (DAL), while Spirit Airlines (SAVEQ) and American Airlines (AAL) impressed me the least.

Fast-forward nearly eight years, and I revisit the US airline sector to assess the four largest companies once again—the Legacy 3 plus Southwest Airlines (LUV). I start today with American, an airline that, in my view, continues to be the riskiest stock to own within its peer group, despite the share price having dropped nearly 70% since the publication of my 2018 study.

A Look at the Macroeconomic Landscape

The airline business is cyclical by nature, and I believe this statement to be intuitive. For this reason, I think that one needs to have an opinion about the direction of the economy before they can place a high-conviction bet on any airline stock.

When looking at my constantly-evolving macroeconomic model, I observe that airline revenues are somewhat reliably predicted by a few leading indicators of economic activity, in the following order of importance: jobless claims (a reflection of the strength in the US job market), consumer expectations of future business conditions (a barometer of travelers' confidence in what lies ahead for the economy), and building permits (likely more of a broad gauge of economic strength rather than something travel-specific).

Below is a chart showing how well these indicators have anticipated revenues for the large US airline group since the mid-1990s through the start of the COVID-19 crisis (as most of us know well, the pandemic broke charts and made meaningful analysis nearly impossible until 2023 or thereabouts), on a four-quarter lag basis. The blue line shows the model predictions, while the orange line represents the smoothed historical annual revenues for the Legacy 3 airlines.

Today, these leading indicators tell a story that is neither strongly bullish nor bearish, likely suggesting that we are sitting somewhere in the middle of the economic cycle. While the landscape may seem to have deteriorated a bit over the last three-plus years, I believe that the pandemic recovery period is to blame for the "denominator distortion".

Otherwise, outside noise in the data, I believe that the environment is one of cruising altitude for the airline sector — we are neither at takeoff nor at final approach for landing. Not surprisingly, therefore, my estimate for the US airline sector revenue growth in 2026 is a modest 3.5% to 4%, roughly matching the consensus expectation for Delta Air Lines, for example (but lagging the 9% forecasted for American).

The recent crisis in the Middle East is the curveball that could add risk to my short-term expectations. This is not so much the case because American Airlines and its US peers are heavily exposed to travel in that part of the world, but rather because delays in settling geopolitical tensions could hurt consumer confidence and drag corporate travel budgets, at least in Europe and the surrounding area.

A Look at American's Fundamentals

If the macro environment does not give me enough reasons to worry about an investment in American Airlines, the same cannot be said about the company's fundamentals. To be clear, I do not see signs of distress in the airline's financial and operating results. Rather, I see a bloated balance sheet relative to the rest of the space, narrow per-unit margins, and operating leverage that would only bode well in a risk-off, early-cycle environment. However, as I explained above, I do not expect to witness such a "blue skies" sector backdrop in the foreseeable future.

The chart below shows how American is still one of the worst major US airlines at producing profits per unit of capacity. Per-unit margin, defined here as PRASM (passenger revenue per unit) minus CASM-ex (operating costs per unit, except fuel and other items) relative to PRASM, is now only marginally better than that of Southwest, a company that has struggled operationally and is currently undergoing a turnaround process, and worse than Delta's and United Airlines' (UAL).

I continue to think that American's tight margins are structural and indicative of inferior core operating economics, reflected in lower yields than Delta and much higher costs than United, which have not been fixed or substantially improved over the past many years. American is more exposed to the lower margin domestic business relative to Delta and United, while crucial personnel costs were above 30% of the company's total annual revenues in 2025 — the only such case among the Legacy 3 players.

Still on operating costs, over the past 16 quarters that cover the more stable post-COVID period, American has incurred average fuel costs per gallon that are higher than the average of the other Big 4 US airlines, with per-unit cost volatility higher than any of its four key peers.

On financial leverage, American continues to stand out as having the riskiest balance sheet (see first chart below). The company's debt plus leases, net of cash, amount to 48% of total assets, about twice as much as United, the runner-up in the peer group comparison. This is barely an improvement over pre-COVID debt levels — arguably an impressive feat in isolation, considering how damaging the pandemic was to the sector, but unimpressive compared to United's leverage ratio drop from 29% then to 25% now.

As a result of poor operating economics and a debt-heavy balance sheet, both relative to the industry, American scores worst at net interest expense coverage (see second chart below), at only 1.2x EBIT-to-interest expense net of interest income. To be clear, I do not think that this represents a significant liquidity or solvency risk for American, assuming that the airline business does not undergo a severe downcycle. It does, however, add to the operating leverage effect, increasing the risk profile: when revenues and operating profits decline, the impact on the bottom line is much more pronounced than it would be for Delta or United.

Takeaways on AAL: Not Junk, Just Risky

In summary, I remain cautiously optimistic about the US airline space in general, understanding that it is particularly sensitive to the economic cycles of consumer behavior and fuel prices, and to geopolitical headwinds. Within the sector, American Airlines ranks near the bottom for a profitable business model and fortress balance sheet, making it not necessarily a junk company or stock, but a riskier one instead.

Should I be wrong about my middle-of-the-cycle assessment, AAL could actually benefit the most within the Legacy 3 peer group from a risk-off environment of rising capacity, occupancy, and airfares. But rather than taking bold bullish bets in an already risky sector, I would much rather ditch AAL and favor investments in DAL in the US or International Consolidated Airlines (ICAGY) abroad.

data.title: Energy Transfer's Most Important Strategic Shift Could Reshape Its Future
data.description:
I previewed Energy Transfer's (ET) Q4 results recently, calling it the partnership's most important earnings report in years. The key question that I asked heading into the report was, how are they going to be approaching capital allocation moving forward, given that they were posting such strong growth numbers, while at the same time, ET had already achieved their leverage target, and had substantial distribution coverage? In this article, I will take a look at the Q4 results, and update my outlook for ET moving forward.

Record Operating Volumes Drive A Massive EBITDA Milestone

ET delivered a solid quarter, with the company posting several records for the fiscal year, including:

  • crude oil transportation volumes, which are up 6% year-over-year,
  • NGL fractionation volumes, which are up 2% year-over-year,
  • record NGL transportation volumes, up 6% year-over-year,
  • total NGL exports, which are up 9% year-over-year,
  • midstream gathered volumes, up 5% year-over-year,
  • record interstate natural gas transport, which was up 7% year-over-year.

This helped the company deliver a record adjusted EBITDA of nearly $16 billion for the full year, which was nearly four times its total growth capital expenditures of $4.5 billion.

Strategic Decisions That Could Shape The Next Decade Of Growth

The company also continued to deliver on numerous strategic initiatives, including:

  • completing open seasons on FGT for two new projects,
  • adding a connection to serve three new power plant loads in Oklahoma, which they expect to come online in Q2 of this year,
  • beginning to flow gas on the first pipeline lateral to a data center campus near Abilene, Texas,
  • announcing increased pipe diameter on the Desert Southwest Expansion Project to increase capacity there,
  • suspending the development of the Lake Charles LNG project,

That last decision was probably the most significant because it showed capital discipline for the management team by avoiding chasing after a riskier project that was a bit out of their reach and instead focusing on their enormous natural gas pipeline infrastructure growth project opportunities. Given the expected demand boom for natural gas in the coming years due to real industrialization, as well as the data center boom in the United States, this seems like a very attractive risk-adjusted way to drive growth for the business and potentially better valuation treatment from the market.

An 11% EBITDA Growth Outlook - But With Massive Capital Spending

Beyond that, the company signaled that they expect adjusted EBITDA to grow by about 11% year-over-year from 2025 to 2026, thanks to new projects that are expected to come online or ramp up in 2026. That being said, capital spending is expected to be very substantial this year too, at between $5 and $5.5 billion on growth spending alone, the vast majority of which is expected to go into natural gas and NGLs, with returns that are expected to come in in the mid-teens.

However, despite this strong growth guidance, management continues to expect to target long-term annual distribution growth in the range of just 3% to 5% per year and reiterated that on the call, saying that they set that growth rate with the expectation that it would be the floor rate that they would be growing their EBITDA and distributable cash flow per unit over time.

Why Natural Gas Is Becoming The Crown Jewel Of The Business

The company's natural gas pipeline footprint, though, is increasingly becoming a crown jewel, as they have a weighted average contract life of 18 years on their natural gas portfolio and continue to add new agreements, including one to provide natural gas to three of Oracle's data centers and power plants in Oklahoma, as well as natural gas to Entergy's facilities, while continuing to work on additional opportunities.

What this ultimately amounts to for ET is that they are increasingly pursuing a strategy similar to Kinder Morgan's (KMI): namely, doubling down on natural gas and related infrastructure investments while growing the distribution at a lower and slower pace than peers.

Why Investors May Still Be Waiting For A Re-Rating

While that is all well and good, and I think it does set up ET for a very successful long-term outlook from both a business durability standpoint and a distribution sustainability and growth standpoint, for anyone who wants the company to achieve its full potential in terms of capital returns in the near term as well as a valuation multiple expansion to be in line with some of its MLP peers, I think they're going to be disappointed.

While its distributable cash flow is expected to cover the distribution at a very conservative level this year, based on analyst consensus estimates, which put the projected coverage ratio at 1.91 times, they could easily grow their distribution at a very aggressive clip to bring it down to a 1.3 to 1.5 times range that peers like MPLX (MPLX) and Plains All American (PAA) are pursuing. However, it appears they want to have a higher coverage ratio because they are investing so aggressively in their growth projects.

The biggest risk for Energy Transfer comes from the fact that it is investing aggressively in growth, which means that capital misallocation is the biggest risk there. While many of the growth projects likely will be highly profitable, especially in the natural gas space, and are positioning the business for long-term durability and potential additional synergy capture through bolt-on acquisitions and even larger mergers and acquisitions, there's still a high risk of capital misallocation, which could dilute unitholder returns over time. This has already been the case with ET in the past, especially compared to its peers, so it remains worth keeping an eye on. That said, ET trades at a big enough discount that some of this misallocation is likely already priced in.

The 7% Yield Is Attractive - But There Is One Catch Investors Should Understand

Thus, ET remains a long-term play that I expect will likely remain undervalued relative to peers for the foreseeable future. However, the 7% yield plus the inflation-meeting-to-beating growth profile that is forecasted still means that the total return profile will likely be decent, especially if we do get some valuation multiple expansion as it becomes increasingly focused on natural gas, which is in favor right now and tends to command a higher multiple from the market. As a result, we remain overweight ET in our 7-8% yielding, below market beta, total return outperforming portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Verizon: Great Performance For The Stock And Even Better Through Options
data.description:
Article Thesis

My last article about Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) was in July last year. I rated it as a “Buy”, with the stock around $41 and a Fair Value estimate of $48.86. Besides, an ~6.5% dividend yield, helped by a modest annual increase, was something rather unusual for a telecom operator.

My strategy was (and is) twofold. First, I have a stock position, which has a total return of ~27.5% since July, which is especially interesting coming from a defensive stock. This is a more passive position, with a mid-to-long term horizon, which was also suitable for dividend growth investors. However, I am not interested in holding it if it appreciated enough and future expected return is low, and I will detail below at what price I target an exit. This is where I differ from dividend growth investors.

Then, I also had a neutral-to-slightly bullish options position through a Diagonal Put, which was paired with a neutral-to-slightly bearish Diagonal Put in AT&T (T), because Verizon was clearly more undervalued than its close telecom peer. With Verizon temporarily going under $40, but with AT&T decreasing even more, I close these two pair positions for a quick profit, with a small loss on the Verizon’s part more than offset by a higher profit on the AT&T’s part. I will detail the mechanics of this pair trade in my future article about AT&T.

With Verizon around or under $40, I now initiated a slightly more bullish strategy through options, a bull Put Spread, which brought an impressive return of ~59% in less than four months. This is the power of options, they bring leverage. And while I also incur losses occasionally, I handle my positions in such a way to lower the probability of these losses (in this case, a really low probability for a telecom operator with a well-covered ~7% dividend yield to go even lower) and to minimize them if they do occur. I will describe below the mechanics of this position and why I don’t repeat it right now.

Business Highlights

Perhaps the most important event in this period since my last article was the appointment in October of a new CEO, Dan Schulman, ex-CEO of PayPal, but also with more than two decades experience in telecom, running AT&T’s consumer division, among others. With five years of small but consistent market share losses, and with the stock down, this was a necessary change. He is more oriented to offer better services for reducing churn instead of short-term price increases and offering a free phone where the service is bad.

Some results were already visible in Q4’25, with both gross and net retail postpaid phone adds growing handily:

But maybe even more important than this is their growth in fiber. They have 30 million homes passed now with fiber, and targeting up to 40 million to 50 million in the medium term. A lot of that growth is coming from the Frontier acquisition. As mentioned in my previous article, I liked that acquisition, not only that they have synergies, not only that they have better credit rating than Frontier and they could retire Frontier’s debt, not only that they can keep up with competition for fiber coverage, but, maybe even more importantly, this brings convergence, cross-sell opportunities, and lower churn rates in the Wireless segment:

A lot of that came from our Frontier acquisition. We have a huge opportunity to grow through that. We are very underpenetrated in the Frontier territory with wireless services… And any time we bundle customers together, there's a 40% reduction in churn versus stand-alone wireless.

Related to fiber, there are also other opportunities now in the business segment, more and more of the hyperscalers looking at “not just building out data centers, but how do you cluster those data centers? How do you tie them together.”

Short-To-Mid Term Outlook

Let’s start with their 2026 guidance:

There were questions about how they could achieve this growth, since Verizon has the highest ARPUs and therefore, maybe the highest prices in the market. They see much of that growth coming from cross-selling, broadband and postpaid convergence. Then, there are cost optimizations, like decommissioning of legacy elements in the network, and perhaps some workforce reduction, and synergies with Frontier.

On top of that, there are share buybacks. If their commitment to dividend increases of 2%-3% per year was expected due to a healthy payout ratio of ~58%, this program with at least $3B buybacks in 2026 and up to $25B for the next three years came as a pleasant surprise:

I think that many analysts were expecting continuous pressure on CapEx, because there were questions like, are you sure $16.5B is enough? But the CEO is confident about that:

When I think of $16 billion to $16.5 billion, it's a tremendous amount of money. Honestly, I don't know if we need all $16 billion to $16.5 billion for our CapEx projects, but I think it's a responsible number to have there.

As Tony said, there are a lot of things that we spent pretty heavily on in the last couple of years. They're predominantly complete. We're almost done with all of our nationwide 5G advanced stand-alone features as well. And so I feel really good about this CapEx envelope. We'll be able to do all the things we need to go do.

Fair Value

I don’t think that a comparison with Verizon’s fiver-year averages is very relevant right now, because Verizon was undervalued most of these last five years. Part of that was related to higher interest rates pressuring this entire capital-intensive industry, and another part was due to Verizon’s perceived weakness with market share losses in wireless and lower fiber coverage. Both of these trends have changed now.

However, we can make a comparison with close telecom peers:

For the telecom industry, I look especially at the EV/EBITDA multiple, because it includes profitability and debt, both very important aspects in this industry:

  • EV/EBITDA TTM = 7.63x, compared to ~8.44x for its peers, implying about ~10% undervaluation.
  • EV/EBITDA FWD = 7.19x, compared to 6.83x for its peers, implying ~5% overvaluation.

By averaging the two, we have Verizon fairly valued today, perhaps only negligibly undervalued.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (discounted dividend model) in two stages, with the following assumptions:

Div growth next 5 years

2.5%

2-3% revenue growth, 58% payout ratio, buybacks

Div growth perpetuity

2.5%

Cost of Equity

7.9%

i) 4.25% risk-free rate, 4.5% cost of equity, 0.82 IYZ Beta

ii) ~5.4% YTM VZ 10-year bond + ~2.5% equity risk

Fair Value

$53.05

My Fair Value estimate increased to $53.05 from $48.86 in July ‘25, mostly due to an increased expected growth for the next 5 years, but also due to some passage of time, with an increased base dividend. Compared to the current price ($50.87), that implies about 4% undervaluation, about the same picture painted by the relative valuation.

Strategy and Risks

Like mentioned in the introduction, I will talk about that pair trade with AT&T in my next article about AT&T, so I want to focus now on the other two strategies

Strategy #1 (Passive): Shares

I am interested to initiate a position in these dividend growth stocks if the sum undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth is higher than an uncertainty threshold. For regulated utilities I use an ~20% threshold implying low uncertainty, but for telecom operators I use ~25%, for a “low+” uncertainty, with their more competitive industry.

Verizon was a "Buy" back in July, and it brought a great total return of ~27.5%, or about 38.5% since the initiation of my position in December 2024. But today we have 4% (undervaluation) + 5.5% dividend yield + 2.5% dividend growth = 12%, clearly below. For now, I still hold my shares (which were about half of my total position), they are a good diversification in a portfolio with a major focus on IT, which came under pressure in January-February.

However, I will sell them if this sum comes under 10%, and this is where I differ from very passive dividend growth investors. With zero undervaluation, and less than 10% combined dividend yield and dividend growth, I see the potential risks outweighing the potential reward. We still need to see those postpaid adds strictly coming due to volume and without aggressive price increases. Then, let’s not forget about these stubbornly high-interest rates. And I am not talking only about a possible re-ignition of inflation or about the FED, but about the market, which thinks differently for now about mid-to-long term economy and 10-year rates.

Above ~$52, I prefer to sell and search for other opportunities. If I can’t find other passive opportunities for a good defensive diversification, I will go more with options strategies, even if more active strategies.

Strategy #2 (Active): Bull Put Spread

Like described through some comments on my previous article, with Verizon around $40 or just slightly below in mid-October, I opened some bull Put Spreads:

  • Short 40 Put Apr’26
  • Long 36 Put Apr’26

Average credit/share was $1.64, and cost was $2.36 (4 – 1.64).

However, on February 2, with Verizon rallying towards $45, I close my Put Spreads for an average of $0.24/share, meaning a profit of $1.4/share, or an impressive ROI of 59% (1.4/2.36) in less than four months. I generally close this kind of position before expiration when there is not much extrinsic value left with both Puts OTM (Out-of-The-Money). In this way, I avoid a price reversal, while that remaining $0.24/share doesn’t justify keeping the position another two to three months until expiration.

However, I did not roll the position further away at ~$45. While today, with Verizon slightly above $50, it’s easy to say that another Put Spread would have been profitable, let’s look a little bit at the risk profile.

Suppose that in February, with Verizon at ~45, I would have sold some 45/41 Put Spreads (short 45 Put, long 41 Put) Jul’26 for ~$1.6 credit/share, or about $2.4 cost/share. But since Verizon just came from under $40, would it really have been difficult to see it at ~$41 again? And, at $41, these Put Spreads would have brought around $1 loss/share, or ~40% negative ROI. Leverage can act both ways!

In conclusion:

  • Somewhere above ~$52 (also depending on short-term trends), I will close my position entirely.
  • Between ~$44 and ~52, I was happy (and still am) with holding my shares, for about half-position (but growing with price appreciation).
  • Between ~$40 and ~44, I will add more (for maybe 0.75x position) through either a Diagonal Put (paired or not with AT&T) or this kind of Put Spreads, but perhaps less aggressive strike prices.
  • Between ~$36 and ~$40, I will add more (for about full position) through more aggressive Put Spreads, exactly like the one above.
  • Under ~$36, and without major fundamental changes, Verizon would be a “Strong Buy” for me, and I would add more (perhaps about 1.25x position) and/or through more aggressive strategies, like buying Call options.

Takeaway

Recent leadership change, fiber expansion, and cross-selling initiatives are driving improved postpaid growth and lower churn, supporting a more positive thesis for Verizon.

My Fair Value estimate increased to $53.05 from $48.86 in July '25, mostly due to an increased expected growth for the next 5 years, but also due to some passage of time, with an increased base dividend.

A passive stock position brought ~38.5% return since December '24, while a more active bull Put Spread position brought ~59% in less than four months, from mid-October 2025 to February 2, 2026.

My overall strategy is:

  • above ~$52, I plan to exit entirely
  • between ~$44–52, I just hold my shares
  • below ~$44, I add via options, with more aggressive strategies under ~$40 and especially under ~$36 barring fundamental changes.

data.title: Broadcom Q1: $100 Billion In AI And Why 'Expensive' Is Relative
data.description:
Introduction

Broadcom (AVGO) had another successful quarter; in fact, they beat their own record on several metrics. It shows in all of the headlines and also in the market's reaction. The stock is up 5% in the after-hours, as I am writing this article, and today I'll break down the result and what to expect in the future, both from the business and the stock.

The first and only time I covered Broadcom on Seeking Alpha was right before Christmas 2025, and then I rated the stock of this company as a Buy, saying that it's not cheap but arguing there may not be many opportunities to buy the stock at a discount. Since then, it is down by about 2%, while the S&P gained 2%, but most likely, if the post-earnings gains are still the same when the market closes today, it will beat the S&P since my last coverage.

This is an earnings coverage, so if you are new to this company and want to learn more about its fascinating business model, what the company does, and where it is growing, I strongly suggest reading my previous coverage. There, I talk about the acquisitions, software, hardware, and AI, margins, and everything else every investor of Broadcom should know about this company.

Broadcom Q1 2026

In this record-breaking quarter, the beat was solid, but this is not the main reason for the optimism. The revenue of $19.31 billion beat the expectations by $170 million and showed the growth of 29.4% year-over-year, but the guidance was the real surprise. The management expects its Q2 revenue to be at about $22 billion, with an adjusted EBITDA of around 68%. This is about $1.5 billion higher than what the analysts wanted to see for Q2:

Our guidance for Q2 is for consolidated revenue of $22 billion, up 47% year-on-year.

Another thing I couldn't skip is the breakdown of the guidance. In my last article, I said AVGO stock has a way to go down if a major correction happens due to the valuation or if the AI demand softens in 2026. According to the guidance, the management expects the semiconductor, and especially AI demand, to be stronger than ever:

We forecast semiconductor revenue of approximately $14.8 billion, up 76% year-on-year. Within this, we expect Q2 AI semiconductor revenue of $10.7 billion, up approximately 140% year-on-year.

This area of their business is way less profitable than the already established software part (I explain it in detail in my previous article). So with the rapidly expanding revenues in AI, as this segment makes up a bigger and bigger part of the overall sales, we expect margins to decline. The good news is that the management projects that the margins are going to be flat year-over-year in the next quarter. The 9% growth year-over-year in infrastructure software revenue is one of the reasons the margins are not dropping:

We expect infrastructure software revenue of approximately $7.2 billion, up 9% year-on-year. For your modeling purposes, we expect consolidated gross margin to be flat sequentially at 77%.

As I already said in my previous article, even though the market gets really upset about declining margins, this metric only makes sense if profitability declines without any major changes in the business model. It usually suggests that the company is unable to ramp up the sales organically or that it overspends on some of the things that were cheaper beforehand, without the ability to pass the cost to the customer (weak pricing power). This is not the case for Broadcom. Its high-margin software business is as strong as ever, gaining momentum as a result of the higher visibility of this company due to the AI demand. But the AI part of the business includes designing the chips, ordering them to be manufactured from a third party, getting them back, and making custom-made systems and racks, using Broadcom’s networking switches with those chips. Only after all of these steps does the company pass the end product to the customer. Even though Broadcom here is mostly a designer, it has its own hardware part, which can't ever be as profitable as software. So essentially, even though the margins will be dropping, this company is much better off, making more money than ever before, and it shows in the current quarter.

Coming back to this quarter, AI revenue is at $8.4 billion, and it's an incredible 106% increase year-over-year (I remind you, they expect this growth to only accelerate in Q2). Their CEO, Hock Tan, even said that he expects to get over $100 billion in AI chip revenue by 2027, which is a huge positive sign. You can read more about him and his execution in my previous piece, but here I would just say this person knows how to invest money and how to execute, so I have a strong confidence in his predictions:

Meanwhile, in scale up, as cluster sizes at our customers expand, we are uniquely positioned to enable these customers to stay on direct attached copper through our 200G SerDes. As we next step up to 400G SerDes in 2028, our XPU customers will likely continue to stay on direct attached copper. And this is a huge advantage as the alternative of going to optical is more expensive and requires significantly more power, reflecting the foregoing factors. Our visibility in 2027 has dramatically improved. Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027.

He explains that this is due to the fact that we went from five major custom XPU customers to six. OpenAI is now a part of it for the 2027 demand increase, which by itself is not a great piece of news, given all of the worries that OpenAI will be able to stand financially to all of the contracts it has signed, but for now, Broadcom benefits from it.

Another incredible piece of news is that Broadcom took care of the supply chain, which includes high-bandwidth memory through 2028, and in my previous article, I said it can be a major reason for faster margin drop (due to the memory shortage and price increase). This by itself creates a moat for Broadcom, as memory is now in real deficit, and those able to get it have a major opportunity to beat their competitors.

Our ability to assure supply in these times of constrained capacity in leading-edge wafers, in high-bandwidth memory and substrates ensures the durability of our partnerships, and we have fully secured capacity of these components for '26 through '28.

Even though everyone is mostly talking about AI now, let's not forget what makes Broadcom a great company. The software segment is what allows this company to invest in R&D and participate very successfully in the AI race. Software revenue for this quarter is at $6.8 billion, with a very modest year-over-year growth of 1%, but the underlying metrics for VMware are saving the situation, and the management believes that AI will only increase the demand for this segment in the future, and as a result, we get the guidance of 9% growth in Q2.

VMware Cloud Foundation, VCF, is the essential software layer in data centers, integrating CPUs, GPUs, storage and networking into a common high-performance private cloud environment. As the permanent obstruction layer between AI software and physical chips, silicon, VCF cannot be disintermediated or replaced. It allows enterprises, in fact, to scale complex generative AI workloads effectively with agility that hardware alone cannot provide. We are confident that the growth in generative and agentic AI will create the need for more VMware, not less.

The annual recurring revenue (ARR) grew by 19%, and total contract value booked exceeded $9.2 billion.

Speaking of financial health, Broadcom remains great at making investors happy. Despite the investments for AI production, the company generated $8 billion in free cash flow this quarter. They returned $10.9 billion to shareholders through a combination of dividends and aggressive share repurchases, which is an incredible piece of news, although they did not forget about themselves as well. The company continues with a pretty heavy SBC (stock-based compensation), which is diluting the shareholders. Broadcom repurchased roughly 23 million shares this quarter, and with an additional $10 billion for the program, they are trying to manage the share count to offset the impact of SBC, although the market would rather they just straightforwardly take it down.

Valuation

Probably the biggest red flag and risk of Broadcom right now is the valuation. Using the non-GAAP net income figures reported over the last year and taking the market cap of about $1.6 trillion (after it supposedly goes up by about 5% today), we get the trailing twelve-month P/E ratio of about 44 times, which is even higher than that for Nvidia (NVDA), which also showed a strong quarter, and investors often like to compare the two. I wouldn't necessarily put them as competitors for my personal portfolio, and as a matter of fact, I hold both of them myself.

At the same time, accounting for the guidance and growth, the picture is much better. Even before this quarter's beat and the massive Q2 guidance, the analyst consensus for FY26 EPS was approximately $10.25. With a diluted share count of 4.94 billion shares and a projected FY 2026 net income of $50.635 billion, forward PE stands at about 30 times, which is a much better level. Moreover, by the PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth), for the last 12 months, this stock has been undervalued by over 70% compared to the sector, and looking forward, it's cheaper by 30%.

Conclusion

Broadcom’s Q1 results proved that the main risk I was worried about the last time around, AI, is more of a positive catalyst for now, and a record $19.3 billion in revenue and a $22 billion guide for the next quarter are tangible proof of that. Another risk the company has taken care of is the critical supply chains for high-bandwidth memory and wafers through 2028. Yet again, an incredible execution.

While the trailing P/E of 44x is not screaming this stock is a Buy, judging by the forward-looking metrics, once again, it may be the best buying opportunity you get unless a major market correction happens. When I rated Broadcom a Buy back in December, I said that cheap is a relative term for a company with this execution and potential.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Ad Deal Buzz Lifts Trade Desk
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

OpenAI explored ad sales with The Trade Desk (TTD) as revenue hits $25B: The ⁠Information. (00:13) Robinhood (HOOD) enters premium credit card battle with new invite-only platinum card. (01:18) Trade court orders Trump administration to start refunding IEEPA tariffs. (02:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

The Trade Desk (TTD) is up 15% in premarket action.

This comes after The Information reported on Wednesday that OpenAI (OPENAI) has held early talks to partner with The Trade Desk (TTD) to help it sell advertising.

OpenAI is also reportedly in talks with other brands, media agencies, and ad tech firms as part of the early ads pilot.

The company recently said that it was beginning to test ads in ChatGPT in the U.S. The test will be for logged-in adult users on the Free and Go subscription tiers.

According to a separate report, the artificial intelligence startup topped $25 billion in annualized revenue as of the ‌end of last month. This is a ​17% increase from the $21.4 billion in annualized revenue that the company generated at the end of the year, the report said.

OpenAI confirmed last Friday that it has raised an astonishing $110B in its latest funding round, with $50B coming from Amazon (AMZN) and $30B apiece from Nvidia (NVDA) and SoftBank (SFTBY).

Robinhood (HOOD) has a new Platinum credit card targeting high-income/wealthy customers.

The card is invite-only, carries a $695 annual fee, similar to other luxury cards, and is positioned to compete with top-end cards like those from American Express (AXP) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM).

Robinhood claims that its benefits are worth over $3,000 annually in value, which comes in the form of complimentary memberships, lifestyle credits, and a "courtesy" Robinhood Gold membership.

"We want to go after ​the legacy players' customers," said Deepak Rao, vice president and general ​manager of Robinhood Money, adding that AmEx was "obviously the benchmark".

The offering competes with American Express Platinum ($895 fee, ~$3,500 perks) and Chase Sapphire Reserve ($795 fee, ~$2,700 perks), using 99.9% pure platinum plating.

A federal trade court judge has ordered the Trump administration to start issuing refunds for the tariffs that were struck down by the Supreme Court last month.

Judge Richard Eaton at the Court of International Trade said, "All importers of record whose entries were subject to IEEPA [International Emergency Economic Powers Act] duties are entitled to the benefit."

The ruling was for a case brought by Atmus Filtration, but Eaton said he will be the only judge who will hear cases pertaining to the refund of IEEPA tariffs.

He noted, "So there is no danger that another judge, even one in this court, will reach any contrary conclusions."

During the hearing for Atmus Filtration's case, the Justice Department's lawyer Claudia Burke said the refund process would be time-consuming as the CBP would have to manually go through millions of import entries.

Judge Eaton said, as reported by The Wall Street Journal, "We live in the age of computers. It must be possible for Customs Service to program its computers so it doesn't need a manual review."

The Trump administration is expected to appeal.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei back in talks with Pentagon about AI deal, FT reports

Nvidia shifts production focus to Vera Rubin amid China-bound H200 setback - report

KOSPI’s greatest recovery: Seoul stages record 12% surge in biggest one-day jump since 2008 crisis

Morgan Stanley to cut about 3% of workforce across key divisions

Catalyst watch:

  • Caterpillar (CAT) Chief Executive Officer Joe Creed and Construction Industries Group President Rod Shurman are expected to participate in a fireside chat at the CONEXPO Conference in Las Vegas.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is up 2% at $76. Bitcoin is up 0.6% at $73,000. Gold is up 0.2% at $5,146.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.4% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Cracker Barrel Old Country Store (CBRL) +8% - Shares surged after posting better-than-expected top- and bottom-line results and narrowing its FY2026 outlook.

Economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 4:30 pm Fed Balance Sheet

data.title: EyePoint: The 'Pre-Readout' Surge Is Hiding A Commercial Reality Check
data.description:
Overview

I wanted to take another look at EyePoint (EYPT) today after it reported its Q4/FY25 earnings. I’ve also been working hard on my Pharma DCF model, so I wanted to assign concrete values to EyePoint’s pipeline assets.

My last EYPT evaluation was in October 2025 and followed encouraging Phase 2 DME data in just a couple of dozen of patients. The company intended to advance their TKI implant, Duravyu (aka EYP-1901), into a Phase 3 program for DME soon (Duravyu has since advanced into two Phase 3 DME trials).

Right now, the market’s focus is on its wet AMD Phase 3 program—which features Duravyu facing aflibercept (standard-of-care) in two non-inferitory clinical trials (LUGANO and LUCIA). LUGANO data was expected in mid-2026. So, we’ve been kind of waiting on LUGANO results as the next big catalyst.

Meanwhile, EYPT has appreciated ~50% since my October “Sell” rating. Because this has happened on no big news, it appears that its stock is simply “running up” before the big readout.

My caution on EYPT is not so much on its clinical prospects, but its market prospects. Specifically, “even in the event Duravyu proves ‘non-inferior’ to aflibercept, its commercial viability will likely remain hazy until long-term outcomes are established.” Duravyu’s main differentiation from standard-of-care is the fact that it can last up to six months without redosing. But I’ve pointed out that eye doctors are already extending EYLEA HD (aflibercept) patients to 16 weeks (despite FDA reluctance to extend dosing intervals further) in the real-world. So, what good then is Duravyu, especially if it may require more “rescue” doses given TKI’s relatively weak efficacy versus powerful anti-VEGFs like aflibercept?

The paragraphs that follow model EyePoint’s DME and wet AMD prospects to arrive at its fair value (minus its preclinical EYP-2301 program that is assumed to have a zero risk-adjusted NPV), and dig deeper into what to expect when it reports LUGANO.

Pipeline

Both DME and wet AMD are crowded markets.

I don’t suspect that TKIs are going to “transform” either market. Best-case scenario—in my opinion—is that TKIs, like Duravyu, are used in stable and predictable eyes. In practice, this is a small portion of each market. Think about it. If existing patients are perfectly stable on EYLEA HD at 16-week intervals, why would they switch to Duravyu just for (best-case) one or two less injections/office visits per year and risk losing efficacy or gaining complications? So there is likely just a very specific subset of patients that would be interested/appropriate (step-therapy) for TKI implants. I modeled for 10% share, which I feel is generous in these large, established, and crowded markets.

Target populations are estimates based on prevalence statistics. Net price is an estimate that uses EYLEA HD WAC as an anchor. One thing that likely deviates from “normal” is Probability of Success. Normally, Phase 3 ophthalmology assets have ~40%-50% Probability of Success. But I feel like Duravyu’s early-stage data isn’t as robust. For instance, we do not yet have data on patients who’ve received multiple Duravyu injections. In another example, the Phase 2 DAVIO2 clinical trial only studied high-vision, maintenance-type eyes with prior anti-VEGF response. Both LUGANO and LUCIA explore both treatment-naive and previously-treated eyes. Treatment-naive eyes are, in theory, “wetter” with uncontrolled disease. Granted, all patients in LUGANO and LUCIA will receive aflibercept induction doses to “dry things up” before being randomized to Duravyu implantation or aflibercept maintenance. Still, "new eyes" are a relatively uncharted territory for Duravyu, which increases the risk of a Phase 3 bust.

I, personally, believe LUGANO and LUCIA patients will require more rescue doses than what we saw in DAVIO2, especially after the aflibercept induction doses wear off.

Anyways, here is what I have modeled for Duravyu in wet AMD:

I’ve assigned a POS of 40% in wet AMD. The Sensitivity Analysis displays how Duravyu’s (wet AMD) value changes when adjustments to launch year and POS assumptions are made. So although I modeled for 40% POS, I also share what Duravyu’s wet AMD value is if the POS assumption is 50%-70%.

DME has a smaller population and Duravyu’s data here is much more limited. Recall that Phase 2 DME was a small, open-label study. So I gave Duravyu a 30% POS here. Duravyu is also expected to launch in DME after AMD.

With my base assumptions in DME and wet AMD set, EyePoint’s stock looks pricey.

Clearly, the market has different assumptions than I do. Perhaps the market (aka “hive mind”) assign higher POS’s? It’s also possible that Duravyu could launch sooner than 2029 (I leaned conservative in this estimate given that the FDA will likely want robust data/long follow-up given these are chronic and common conditions). Maybe it’s a combination of things?

In any event, this is how I value EYPT.

You may have noticed that I model that EyePoint doesn’t have enough cash (“over $300M” per its last update) to get it to profitability (est. for 2032). This is also consistent with management’s cash runway estimate of “into Q4 2027.” Although this may be sufficient to fund both programs, it’s likely not enough for a launch.

To make up for this deficit, I modeled for 2.25% annual dilution for six years, which actually isn’t much for a pre-commercial biotechnology nearing marketization. Ironically ("ironic" because “cash” and “shares outstanding” should theoretically cancel each other out), this adjustment increases EYPT’s fair value because its current share price ($17.70) is much higher than my estimated share price ($11.78).

Management Commentary

EyePoint has just recently highlighted Duravyu’s supposed multi-MOA (VEGF, PDGF, IL-6 via JAK1) in an attempt to differentiate it beyond mere interval extension (which has been squeezed due to modern “treat-and-extend” practice). In line with my 10% peak share estimates, management expects prescribers to trial Duravyu first in high-burden patients rather than as a first-line.

But if one extrapolates from the Phase II data, I think that at the beginning, where most doctors will try it, is their patients who are being treated more frequently than they would like, every 4 weeks, every 6 weeks, every 8 weeks. I think that will be the initial adoption of it. And as doctors get comfortable with its therapeutic profile and its safety, I think it will get expanded.

So this is being treated as a “base case,” which is smart because convincingly “beating” a powerful anti-VEGF on vision outcomes will not be an easy task.

Ocular Therapeutics (OCUL) just recently found this out. Ocular is also testing a long-duration TKI implant, Axpaxli, in wet AMD. Although the trial met its superiority-focused endpoint, some were not impressed with the results.

The SOL-1 Phase 3 study tested only one low dose (2mg) of EYLEA versus Axpaxli (after loading)—so blatantly suboptimal anti-VEGF treatment against a long-duration TKI.

Even with "both hands tied behind its back," suboptimally-dosed aflibercept put up a decent fight:

If anything, SOL-1 shows how powerful just one low dose of aflibercept is at drying wet eyes. Of course, results may look much different when Axpaxli actually faces off with (what is closer to being the) standard-of-care in the Phase 3 SOL-R—which will evaluate aflibercept 2mg every eight weeks in one arm and EYLEA HD every 24 weeks in the other.

EyePoint wasn’t as ambitious as Ocular against "aflibercept with both hands tied behind its back,” so both of its wet AMD trials evaluate non-inferiority primary endpoints only. The upcoming LUGANO results will feature Duravyu against aflibercept 2mg every eight weeks and measure visual acuity. There are many ways for Duravyu to underwhelm even in the event it meets non-inferiority on visual acuity. For instance, if too many rescue doses are required to keep Duravyu afloat, its "dosing interval advantage" could quickly erase itself against standard-of-care.

EyePoint is preparing to “go it alone” in the US. They just hired a CCO and lauded its “41,000 square foot cGMP manufacturing facility” that is prepping to support commercial supply.

Final Words

EyePoint is in a good spot (financially and operationally), so it’s not surprising to see some price appreciation (aka speculation) ahead of pivotal LUGANO data given the massive TAMs in DME and wet AMD.

That said, I’m a bit wary of the risk/reward at these prices. EyePoint’s $1.5B market cap seems to already price in a good deal of success and these are tough markets. And if TKI implants really are of use in these conditions, Duravyu could feasibly split this niche market (following inadequate responses to an anti-VEGF) with Ocular’s Axpaxli—making my 10% peak share estimates even harder to swallow.

So I’m still at a “Sell” on EYPT. In my view, either TKI implants will "bomb" against standard-of-care or they will demonstrate just enough efficacy to be relevant in select patients. This just isn’t a compelling risk/reward scenario for me.

Risks to Thesis

Some things that could change my mind include compelling Phase 3 data that clearly demonstrates Duravyu superiority over EYLEA HD in some meaningful way beyond its shrinking dosing interval advantage (i.e., special antifibrotic or neuroprotective benefits that management cited), FDA approval with favorable labeling, and evidence of real-world adoption in DME/wet AMD.

data.title: Allianz: Strong 2025 Results, Capital Strength And Supportive Guidance Lead Us Back To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Following Allianz's (ALIZF) (ALIZY) Q4 and Fiscal Year 2025 results, we are back to comment on the company's results. Since our last update, Allianz's share price has been flat (Fig. 1). As a reminder, our neutral rating was mainly driven by a fair valuation. In Q3, the company delivered solid Q3 results with record EBIT, supportive EPS growth, and an upgraded 2025 operating profit guidance.

Before moving on to our forward-thinking view on Allianz, we have also reviewed Zurich Insurance Group and AXA SA's latest numbers. We report that all insurers have strong capital positions (with high Solvency II ratio capital requirements) and resilient earnings across life, non-life, and reinsurance lines. In our coverage, all companies maintain stable credit ratings and outlooks. Looking at the segment, insurers benefited from a prolonged higher-interest-rate environment that boosted investment income (thanks to higher yields) and eased pressure on life insurance guarantees. Additionally, the Property & Casualty segment has performed well, driven by higher pricing and better cost management, resulting in a pickup in profitability.

Fig 1

Allianz Results and Our Positive View

Allianz's 2025 results were impressive. Starting with the annual reporting, the company's business volume increased by 8.11% to €186.9 billion (Fig. 2). All divisions contributed to the solid results. Allianz's core operating profit increased by 8.4%, the highest in its history. Looking at the P&L, the company reported a net profit of €11.1 billion, with EPS up 12.5% to €28.61. In our last update, we were estimating a 2026 EPS of €30. This upside relative to our previous assessment could represent a key catalyst for a potential change in our rating. However, before moving forward with our positive view, we also report key numbers from Allianz's Q4 results. Group operating profit reached €4.29 billion (Fig. 3), in line with Wall Street consensus estimates. On the segment results, P&C operating profit reached €2.13 billion with a CoR of 93.6%. Nat cats were 30 basis points lighter and had a 10-basis-point lower expense ratio than cones. On a positive note, the operating investment result was a 9% beat. This beat was entirely due to interest accretion. Lastly, the L&H segment's operating profit was €1.36 billion, with a gross CSM stock of €55.7 billion.

Source: Allianz FY 2025 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we Positive?

Allianz 2025 performance highlights the group’s ability to generate sustainable value. Management also continues to advance cross-selling initiatives across the platform. However, the key metric we continue to monitor closely remains the net retention rate, with a lower churn rate (Fig. 4). Aside from that, Allianz’s 2024-2027 Lifting Ambitions plan targets a core EPS CAGR of 7-9%, a ROE above 17%, and a payout of 75%. Allianz delivered 12.5% core EPS growth, ROE of 18.1%, and a 79% total payout. The company is delivering on and accelerating the main levers of its strategic plan.

Fig 3

The results were supported by a 7-ppt Solvency II beat versus the Wall Street consensus expectation. This was largely driven by model adjustments, alongside stronger-than-expected capital returns. Management also expects the Solvency II review to add 5-10 basis points in Q1 2026, while additional solvency benefits from markets such as LATAM and the UK. The Fiscal Year dividend of €17.1 per share was in line with estimates (Fig. 4). However, the announced €2.5 billion share buyback exceeded both consensus and our internal estimate of €2 billion. While someone might say the incremental yield is modest, Allianz aims to deliver at least last year's DPS, and consensus did not forecast this level of buyback. Therefore, there is solid downside protection, combined with a yield of 4.75%.

Fig 4

The company’s 2026 operating profit guidance is set at a mid-point of €17.4 billion (± €1 billion) (Fig. 5). This reflects Allianz’s consistent execution track record. Given this visibility, the guidance could lead to modest low-single-digit upgrades to market expectations and, in our view, should support a constructive share price reaction.

Fig 5

Lastly, business-wise, there are many moving parts to report. 1) P&C attritional loss ratio improved. In numbers, Allianz’s undiscounted attritional loss ratio improved from 71.8% in 2024 to 71.3% in 2025. This was due to supportive performance in motor and SMEs businesses. The company continues to have a good retail P&C pricing momentum. In numbers, P&C premiums rose by 8.2%. Motor claims pricing is above the CPI inflation rate. In the asset management segment, net flows will remain positive, with the management reporting that net inflows were double-digit in Q1 2026. On the AI, the company is working to reduce fraud with the support of new applications.

Valuation

In our last update, we assumed core EPS growth of 8% on 2026 results. We also reported that Allianz's 2025 earnings forecast was rising, and "we would typically also raise our 2026 numbers, which in turn would push our valuation higher. However, Allianz's recent prints, while solid, do point to some deceleration, so we are not entirely convinced this is the right step at this stage." This is no longer the case. The company released a supportive 2026 outlook and comprised a P&C operating profit of €9 billion, L&H operating profit of €5.7 billion, and AM operating profit of €3.5 billion. We have made only limited changes to our estimates; however, we have slightly increased our EPS growth assumption by 10%, starting from a higher 2025 core EPS base than in our previous projections. Therefore, we reached a 2026 EPS of €31.5. Applying a P/E target of 12.5x (Fig. 6), we derive a stock price of €393.75 per share ($45.8 in ADR), moving Allianz back to a buy rating estimate. Allianz trades in the U.S. through an ADR, which is equivalent to 1/10 of a German (EUR) share. There is a 10% capital appreciation opportunity, combined with a total yield of 6.55% (4.75% in dividends and the rest in buyback yield).

Fig 6

Risks

We have long covered Allianz, so downside risks include higher regulatory capital requirements and changes in equity markets. Allianz's downside risks are lower demand for insurance products. Tesla has offered its own insurance products to lower Tesla insurance coverage costs for its clientele. So, US insurance companies such as Lemonade already decided to cut insurance costs. This is a risk to report. We also report accounting changes, and changes in corporate tax rates might affect the company's P&L. Allianz is managing its Nat Cat losses well. Still, there are other risks to consider, such as cyber and terrorist attacks.

Conclusion

Allianz offers solid downside protection in a challenging operating environment. In uncertain times like this, insurers with strong capital positions and a positive outlook should be added to a portfolio. With growth ahead and a supportive evolution in ROE, we are buyers again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mitek Systems: How Its AI Fortress Protects 50% Of U.S Banks
data.description:
Mitek Systems (MITK) launched its Check Fraud Defender which is AI cloud platform inspecting Cheque forgery in real-time, yet the company's real value is in its constirium which covers over 50% of U.S checking banks, as in the traditional system if a bank discovered a fraud attempt of a forged check it will only protect itself but with using MITK's consortium, this info will be sent to its cloud platform within a fraction of second so all other banks in the constirium will know about it. This advantage creates a strong moat for Mitek and makes it harder for competitors, as the more banks join the consortium, the more powerful the system becomes and also the less able fraudsters are to penetrate it. This makes Mitek an indispensable strategic partner for banks and pay it subscriptions for life.

Overview

During Q1 2026, the company recorded SaaS revenues of 21% growth YoY, which represents 43% of total revenues of the last 12 months and suggests that the company is moving away from the old licensing model in favor of a SaaS model, justifying giving it a higher valuation than it is.

The reports indicate that global digital identity is expected to reach $32 billion by 2028. While the financial fraud sector is undergoing a difficult situation, GenAI made it easier for fraudsters to devise new ways to bypass security systems at even lower cost, leading to an attack flood on banks. According to Deloitte, AI fraud in the U.S. could reach $40 billion by 2027, demonstrating the urgent need for financial institutions to have the latest protection systems.

Despite the decline in the use of paper checks worldwide, cheque fraud is on the rise as banks have not paid it enough attention compared to protecting credit cards and bank transactions. Gangs use what is called Check Washing through a chemical process, as they erase the beneficiary name or the amount from the real check and use a fake one. The Defender solution performs an advanced visual inspection of over 20 attributes of the real check via AI.

Identity Solutions

The company also offers its digital identity products such as MiVIP, IDLive Face, and IDLive Voice, which are among the best in their class, as Mitek can determine whether the user's face or voice is for a real person or not. What distinguishes MITK's strategy is its focus on reducing customer contact, which attracts more customers to the partners; at the same time it raises the protection walls against fraudsters. The management stated that it reached a 90% automation rate, which means every 9 out of 10 users can open bank accounts without human review, reducing operation costs significantly and making switching to Mitek's products an obvious decision for any bank manager.

Financials & Outlook

In Q1, the company announced total revenues of $44.2 million with 19% growth YoY exceeding expectations of $42.4 million, driven by Fraud & Identity solutions which represents 57% of total business with $25.5 million and 30% growth YoY, meaning that MITK's core growth engine significantly accelerates compared to the traditional check verification segment, which also showed resilience and stability with revenues of $18.8 million. The Adjusted EBITDA also grew by %69, this significant growth reflects operating leverage as when revenue increases, the costs will remain constant at the same rate, thus increasing profits, as the profit margin reached 30% vs 21.1% last year.

The company also turned a loss of $4.6 million last year, achieving $2.8 million in the same period, demonstrating Mitek's ability to achieve real profits.

Moreover, the management raised its guidance for 2026, as it expects revenues between $187 and $197 million and also raised its guidance for Adjusted EBITDA to nearly 30%, indicating that the company has a clear vision of its backlog orders, mitigating the risk of sudden profit decline. Additionally, it places it among top tier software companies that apply the 40 Rule, enhancing its valuation.

Value Per Share

MITK trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 11x. However, the sector median is approximately 14x. Considering the full year guidance expecting average revenues of 192 million and adjusted EBITDA nearly 30%. I will assume EBITDA of $57 million. Companies that achieve rule of 40 usually trade at higher multiples; however, by applying a multiple of 13x to put a margin of safety, The EV will approach $741 million, adding cash & equivalents of $191.8 million and subtracting total debt of $159.2 million, the equity value comes to $774 million or $17 per share, representing potential upside of 15%.

Risks To Consider

Although the promising future, the AI-identity field is crowded and rapidly evolving, as modern technologies today could be outdated tomorrow. Therefore, it may be overtaken by fraudsters using newer AI techs. However, Mitek is aware of this risk and plans to increase its R&D spending sequentially during 2026 in order to protect its economic moat.

In addition, biometric privacy laws are strict and require explicit written consent, this law imposes harsh fines of up to $5000 per violation, and the company already found itself embroiled in some of these lawsuits, such as a case with HyerCar, forwhich Mitek was providing it a service as a third-party behind the scenes.

Final Thoughts

Given Mitek's excellent quarter and guidance, we find that the upside potential far outweighs the downside risks, plus the company's buyback program of $50 million signals the upside trend. However, we need to ensure the continuation of SaaS revenue growth, which reached 43% during Q1. Therefore, its approach to the 50% mark can force the market, giving it higher multiples as a SaaS company.
